title: Performing VT to a Procedure: Parameters and Standardization
narrationScript:
A Level One visual inspector does not decide how to inspect. The procedure decides. Your skill is executing that procedure exactly, every part, every shift, so the result does not depend on whose eyes were on duty. This lesson is about reading a procedure the way a pilot reads a checklist, and about the parameters someone selected before you ever picked up the flashlight.

Start with why the parameters exist. Somebody, usually a Level Three working with engineering, decided what this examination must find. That is the inspection objective. A final acceptance exam on a structural weld is hunting cracks, undercut, and porosity against a code limit. An in-process check on a root pass is hunting incomplete fusion before the next pass buries the evidence. An in-service look at a pump is hunting corrosion and wear. Different objectives drive different lighting, different access, and different acceptance criteria. The objective is written down so the whole team hunts the same animal.

Next, checkpoints. A checkpoint is a defined moment in the work sequence when inspection happens. On a weld job, that might be fit-up before the arc strikes, then the root pass, then selected interpass checks, then the finished weld. Some checkpoints are hold points. Work stops, and it may not continue until the inspection is done and signed. Others are witness points, where the customer is invited to watch, but work may proceed if they waive it or do not show. Blow through a hold point and you have bought back-charges, cut-outs, and a very bad meeting. Know which checkpoints apply before the job starts.

Sampling plans answer the question, how many do we look at. Critical work is often one hundred percent examination. Every unit, every weld. High-volume production may use a sampling plan instead. First-article inspection examines the first piece off a new setup in detail before the run continues. Periodic sampling pulls a part every hour or every batch. Lot-based plans pull a stated sample from each lot, and the lot is accepted or rejected based on how many rejects appear in that sample. Here is the rule that matters most. When a sample fails, you do not just swap out the one bad part. The plan typically expands the sample or rejects the lot, because one reject in a small sample means the lot probably hides more. Follow the plan as written, and never let anyone hand-pick the parts you sample.

Patterns are how you guarantee coverage. The human eye does not sweep a surface evenly without help. So we scan in a deliberate pattern, like mowing a lawn. Overlap each pass so nothing slides between two looks. On big surfaces, mark a grid and complete one block at a time, checking blocks off as you go. On pipe, work in circumferential bands or a continuous spiral. Keep your eye within twenty-four inches of the surface, and keep your viewing angle at least thirty degrees off the surface, because flatter than that, detection falls apart. Move at a pace that lets your eye lock on and resolve, not a pace that just gets you to lunch.

All of this lives in the documented procedure. A typical visual testing procedure gives you a scope, the references it answers to, personnel qualification requirements, an equipment list, lighting requirements, surface preparation, the technique with distances and angles, the acceptance criteria, and the reporting requirements. The lighting paragraph states a minimum, commonly five hundred lux for general work and one thousand lux for critical examination, measured at the surface with a calibrated light meter. Many procedures must also be demonstrated. A common demonstration is resolving a fine line, no wider than one thirty-second of an inch, on a neutral gray test card placed where the work will be. If the system, meaning your eyes, the light, the distance, and the angle, cannot resolve that line, you fix the setup before you inspect hardware.

Finally, standardization. A test standard can be a document, like a code or a specification. It can also be a physical thing, a reference you use to set up and verify the test. Surface roughness comparators, weld replica gauges, resolution cards, and specimens with known discontinuities all standardize the examination, so two qualified inspectors in two cities get the same answer from the same part. Calibrated instruments are part of that promise. A light meter with an expired calibration sticker proves nothing.

Treat the procedure as the contract for your eyes. Read it before the job. Verify the conditions it demands. Execute the pattern it specifies. Record what it tells you to record. That is performing visual testing to a specific procedure, and it is the whole difference between an inspection and a glance.

sections.heading: The Procedure Decides, You Execute
sections.body:
At Level I you perform specific examinations and evaluations to written instructions — and that is by design. The selection of parameters is engineering work, done before the job by a Level III or the responsible engineer, flowing down from the code, the contract, and the drawing. The procedure fixes:

- the **objective** — what the examination must find,
- the **checkpoints** — when in the work sequence you look,
- the **sampling plan** — how many units you look at,
- the **pattern** — how you cover the surface,
- the **conditions** — lighting, surface preparation, viewing distance and angle, equipment,
- the **acceptance criteria** and the **report format**.

None of those are yours to improvise. If a parameter cannot be met — the light meter reads low, access will not allow the required angle — you stop and report it up. Quietly working around an unmet parameter invalidates the examination just as surely as missing the discontinuity would. The procedure is the contract between your eyes and everyone who relies on them.

sections.heading: Objectives and Checkpoints
sections.body:
**Inspection objectives** answer one question: what must this examination find? A final-acceptance examination of new structural welds hunts cracks, undercut, porosity, and profile problems against a workmanship standard. An in-process check of a root pass hunts incomplete fusion before the next pass buries the evidence. A receiving inspection screens mill product for laminations and seams. An in-service examination hunts corrosion, wear, and fatigue cracking and compares against the last baseline. The objective drives everything downstream — lighting, access, aids like magnifiers and mirrors, and which acceptance criteria apply — which is why it is stated, never assumed.

**Inspection checkpoints** put the looks at defined moments in the work sequence. A typical weld sequence: examine **fit-up** — root opening, bevel angle, alignment, cleanliness — before the arc strikes; examine the **root pass**; examine selected **interpass** stages, including interpass cleaning; examine the **finished weld** after slag removal and cooling. Two checkpoint flavors matter contractually:

- a **hold point** stops the work — production may not continue until the inspection is performed and signed off;
- a **witness point** invites the customer or third party to observe, but work may proceed if they waive it or do not appear.

Blowing through a hold point can mean cutting finished work apart to recreate the inspection. Know your checkpoints before the job starts, and get each one documented as you clear it.

sections.heading: Sampling Plans: How Many Do We Examine?
sections.body:
A sampling plan states how much of the product gets examined, and it is selected for the risk of the hardware — never for the time available.

- **100 percent examination** — every unit, every weld, every surface in scope; standard for safety-critical and code work.
- **First-article inspection** — the first piece from a new setup, die, or process examined in detail before the run continues; it catches setup errors while the count is still one.
- **Periodic or frequency-based sampling** — one unit every hour, every pallet, or every shift start; common for stable, high-volume production.
- **Lot-based sampling** — a stated sample drawn from each lot, with accept and reject numbers taken from industry sampling standards; the lot stands or falls on the sample.

Two field rules keep sampling honest. First, the sample must be random or drawn exactly as the plan directs — no cherry-picking clean parts, and no letting the shop hand you the ones they like. Second, a reject in the sample is never solved by swapping out the single bad part. One reject in a small sample is statistical smoke: the plan will direct you to expand the sample or reject the whole lot. Record exactly which serial numbers you examined so the sample itself is traceable. If the plan is unclear, the question goes to your Level II or III — not to a guess.

sections.heading: Inspection Patterns: Guaranteeing Coverage
sections.body:
Eyes do not sweep evenly without a plan; they jump, and whatever sits between jumps goes unseen. An inspection pattern turns looking into coverage:

- **Raster passes** — parallel, overlapping sweeps across a surface, like mowing a lawn; each pass overlaps the previous one so no strip escapes between fields of view.
- **Grid segmentation** — large surfaces such as tank shells get marked into a numbered grid; complete one block at a time and check each block off. The grid is your proof of coverage and your place-keeper after every interruption.
- **Circumferential bands or a spiral** — for pipe, tubing, and shafts; advance the band a controlled amount each revolution.
- **Weld scanning** — work the joint systematically: each toe, the cap face, and the root when accessible, on both sides, before moving down the weld.

While the pattern moves, the viewing envelope holds: eye within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface, viewing angle not less than 30 degrees to the surface, and the procedure's minimum illumination on the area being viewed. Pace matters too — scan slowly enough for the eye to lock on and resolve detail, not just pass over it. Where access breaks the envelope, that is what mirrors, magnifiers, and borescopes are for; otherwise the area is documented as not examined.

sections.caption: Patterns guarantee coverage: overlapping raster passes with grid marking on plate, circumferential bands on pipe, and the direct-viewing envelope of 24 in. (600 mm) at not less than 30 degrees.

sections.heading: Inside the Documented Procedure
sections.body:
A written VT procedure reads much the same across most shops, and learning the skeleton means you can find anything fast:

- **Scope** — what hardware and what stage of work the procedure covers.
- **References** — the codes, specifications, and contract documents it answers to.
- **Personnel** — required certification level and a current vision examination.
- **Equipment** — light sources, meters, gauges, mirrors, magnifiers; calibration requirements for anything that measures.
- **Lighting** — the minimum illumination at the examination surface and how to verify it.
- **Surface preparation** — the condition the surface must reach before examination counts.
- **Technique** — direct or remote viewing, maximum eye-to-surface distance, minimum viewing angle, scan pattern, and aids.
- **Acceptance criteria** — the limits results are evaluated against, stated or referenced.
- **Reporting** — which form, which fields, who signs, where records go.

Two habits make you the technician supervisors trust. Work to the **revision invoked for the job** — procedures change, and yesterday's revision may carry yesterday's lighting requirement. And **verify the demonstrable requirements on site**: measure the light at the surface, confirm the meter's calibration sticker, and when the procedure requires a resolution demonstration, prove the whole system — eyes, light, distance, angle — before hardware is judged. If any element cannot be met, the examination stops until the responsible Level III disposes of the conflict in writing.

sections.caption: The standard skeleton of a written VT procedure — every examination parameter is selected and recorded before the first look at hardware.

sections.title: Numbers You Verify, Never Assume
sections.body: Common code practice for direct visual examination: minimum **500 lux (50 fc)** for general examination and **1,000 lux (100 fc)** for critical or final examination, measured **at the surface** with a **calibrated meter**; eye within **24 in. (600 mm)**; viewing angle **not less than 30 degrees** to the surface; remote systems demonstrated to resolve detail no wider than about **1/32 in. (0.8 mm)** — a fine line on a neutral gray card is the classic target. Your procedure's numbers govern: read them, then verify them at the workpiece.

sections.heading: Test Standards and Standardization
sections.body:
The word **standard** does double duty, and a Level I needs both meanings. A test standard as a **document** is the code, specification, or company standard that defines how the examination is performed and judged. A test standard as **hardware** is a physical reference used to set up, verify, or judge the test:

- **Resolution targets** — a fine line or pattern on a neutral gray card, used to demonstrate that the examination system resolves the required detail under actual job conditions.
- **Comparators** — surface-roughness comparator plates and weld replica gauges that put a known geometry next to the unknown one.
- **Known-discontinuity specimens** — parts with documented flaws used to confirm a technique finds what it claims to find.
- **Calibrated instruments** — light meters and measuring gauges with current, traceable calibration.

What standardization buys is **repeatability**: two qualified examiners, two shifts, two sites — same part, same answer. It also catches quiet degradation. Lamps dim with age, batteries sag, lenses film over, and a check against a physical standard exposes all of it before hardware gets judged under bad conditions. The habit is cheap: verify the light at the surface, verify the calibration stickers, run the demonstration when required, and only then start the pattern. An examination you cannot reproduce is an opinion.

figures.title: Anatomy of a Written VT Procedure
Inspection Patterns and the Direct-Viewing Envelope

checkQuestions.question: Production reaches a hold point listed in the inspection plan, but the inspector is two hours away. What happens?
options: Work continues and the inspection is performed when the inspector arrives
Work stops until the inspection is performed and signed off
The foreman may waive the hold point to protect the schedule
The hold point automatically converts to a witness point
checkQuestions.explanation: A hold point is a mandatory stop: work may not proceed past it until the required inspection is performed and documented. Only the authority that imposed it can waive it — never production.

checkQuestions.question: Your lot-based sampling plan calls for examining 8 castings from a lot of 80, and one of the 8 is rejectable. The correct response is to:
options: Replace the bad casting with a good one and accept the lot
Accept the lot, since one reject out of eight is reasonable
Follow the plan: expand the sample or reject the lot as it directs
Re-examine only the castings that look suspect
checkQuestions.explanation: One reject in a small sample suggests more hiding in the lot. The plan states what a sample reject triggers — typically expanded sampling or lot rejection — and substituting parts defeats the statistics entirely.

checkQuestions.question: Why do raster scan passes overlap?
options: To finish the surface faster
So no strip of surface falls between adjacent fields of view
To double-check indications found on the previous pass
To compensate for low lighting
checkQuestions.explanation: The eye covers a limited field on each pass. Overlapping passes guarantee that the edges of adjacent fields of view share coverage, so nothing slides through between them unexamined.

checkQuestions.question: Before a remote examination, the procedure requires resolving a fine line on a neutral gray card placed at the work location. The point of this demonstration is to:
options: Calibrate the light meter
Prove the complete viewing system can resolve the required detail under actual examination conditions
Test the examiner's color vision
Verify the surface preparation of the part
checkQuestions.explanation: The demonstration exercises the whole chain — optics, lighting, distance, angle, and the examiner's eyes — against a known target where the work will actually happen. If the line cannot be resolved, the setup gets fixed before hardware is examined.

checkQuestions.question: Which of these is a physical test standard used to standardize a visual examination?
options: A surface-roughness comparator plate
The procedure revision log
The inspector's certification card
The nonconformance report form
checkQuestions.explanation: A comparator plate is a physical reference: it puts a known surface next to the unknown one so different examiners judge roughness the same way. The other items are records, not references used to standardize the test.

references: Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 — direct and remote visual examination practice (generic reference)
Industry sampling standards such as ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 (generic reference)
SNT-TC-1A — Level I performs examinations to written instructions
